event_blocks.summary: A full moon rises over a dark landscape as a male voice asks, "Do you want to see what the world is really like?" followed by an affirmative "Yes." The scene shifts to a blonde woman in a pink car driving through a suburban street, while a deeper male voice reflects, "I find the world a complicated place." A man in a red vest is seen in a tense, dimly lit interior as a countdown begins with "Three." A man in a dark coat stands atop an illuminated structure against a dark sky, as the voice continues, "We imagine a future," and the countdown reaches "Two." Armored figures walk through a fiery, war-torn environment, emphasizing danger, as the voice concludes, "And our imaginings horrify us," followed by "One" and an explosion. A man in a suit at a table is seen as a female voice shouts, "Stop it!" A red energy explosion occurs in a dark room, and an assertive male voice declares, "There's gonna be some changes made." A smiling couple is shown, and a softer male voice expresses, "I love people so much." A man in a red and white outfit carries a child through a lush landscape, while the voice continues, "It's hard for me to be alone," followed by a roar/scream. A man in a white shirt lies on a bed in distress as a voice asks, "Anita, you here?" People splash joyfully in water, and a reassuring male voice says, "People wanna love you." A man stands triumphantly on a snowy mountain peak as the voice advises, "You should let 'em love all of you." A woman in black-and-white in a vintage setting looks concerned, and an authoritative male voice states, "Humans only have one ending." A chaotic, neon-lit cityscape shows people running in panic, and a determined female voice asserts, "Ideas live forever." A figure falls through a stormy sky as an encouraging female voice begins, "Imagine knowing that you could do something..." A woman in a white dress is comforted as the voice finishes, "...that only you could do." A woman in a mechanical headpiece is shown as a deep male voice declares, "The world will remember this day." The sequence ends with a man in a dark suit surrounded by glowing objects as a calm female voice instructs, "Close your eyes. Now, feel."

event_blocks.summary: The Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, confidently commanded the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, adorned with flowing drapes and a large Oscar statue. He opened with a humorous monologue, referencing the length of 'Killers of the Flower Moon' by suggesting one could drive to Oklahoma and solve the murders within its runtime, eliciting laughter and applause from the formally dressed Oscars Audience. He then introduced Bradley Cooper, acknowledging his 'best picture nominee with Maestro' and playfully questioning his frequent dates with his mother, which drew hearty laughter. The host also shared Oscar trivia about Jodie Foster and Robert De Niro's 'Taxi Driver' nominations from 48 years prior. The Blonde Woman in Pink Car, now in a navy blue satin dress, clapped enthusiastically, while the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with sparkly sunglasses laughed heartily. The host announced Lily Gladstone's historic nomination as the first Native American for Best Actress for 'Killers of the Flower Moon,' met with significant applause, and humorously recounted her near-career change to tracking murder hornets. He then listed first-time acting nominees, including Emily Blunt and Sterling K. Brown, and noted the unprecedented three foreign language films nominated for Best Picture, two starring Sandra Hüller, whose roles in 'Anatomy of a Fall' and 'The Zone of Interest' were humorously described as 'rom-coms' in Germany, drawing hearty laughter. The host announced a future addition to the Oscars categories: 'achievement in casting,' and joked about actors winning awards for parts others didn't get. He directly addressed 'Stephen,' the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, and his wife, Kate Capshaw, acknowledging their significant donations during the recent actors' and writers' strike. The host reflected on the strike, humorously questioning if 'Mario Lopez makes a sound' if a movie premieres without actors to promote it, and stated that actors no longer have to worry about AI replacing them, but rather 'younger, more attractive people.' He also quipped that AI could have written 'Transformers Rise of the Beasts.' The Woman with Blue Gemstone Jewelry nodded appreciatively throughout, and the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses laughed heartily at various jokes, maintaining a celebratory and glamorous atmosphere under the warm, theatrical lighting.

event_blocks.summary: The Woman in Black Dress on Stage, holding an envelope, announced Da’Vine Joy Randolph as the winner of the Oscar for Best Supporting Actress, stating, "10 years after that, giving this transcendent performance on the big screen. We don't just see Sophia's strength, but we see her vulnerability as well. And you, you have earned this full circle moment, my friend." The audience erupted in thunderous applause and cheers, with Jodie Foster, the Blonde Woman in Pink Car, visibly emotional and wiping tears, while the Woman in Sparkling Silver Dress and the Woman in Pink Sequined Dress clapped enthusiastically. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, radiant in a shimmering light-blue gown with voluminous, ruffled shoulders, walked to the stage amidst triumphant, celebratory background music, embraced the Oscar statuette from the Woman in Black Dress on Stage, and returned through the still-applauding audience. On the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, Da’Vine Joy Randolph, visibly moved and tearful, delivered an emotional acceptance speech, declaring, "God is so good," and recalling her mother guiding her to theater. She expressed gratitude to her mother, "all the people who have stepped in my path and has been there for me," and "all the women who have been by my side," specifically naming Colleen Camp, Barbara Broccoli, Tracy Brennan, and Sarah Fargo, before humorously thanking her publicist. Her voice trembled as she powerfully declared, "I just need to be myself," recalling a past moment when she was "the only Black girl in that class" and was told, "you told me I was enough." She concluded, "I am forever grateful. I pray to God that I get to do this more than once," her words met with sustained applause.

event_blocks.summary: The Oscars stage, decorated with art-deco columns and screens displaying animated characters from 'The Boy and the Heron', 'Elemental', 'Nimona', 'Robot Dreams', and 'Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse', was illuminated as an upbeat orchestral score played and the audience, including a woman in a white dress and a man in a dark suit with glowing objects, applauded. The Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, entered the stage and delivered a comedic monologue, joking about absent nominees and the 'Adapted Screenplay' category, which drew laughter from the audience, including a man in a tuxedo with glasses and a woman with blue gemstone jewelry. He then introduced Melissa McCarthy, in a black sequined blazer and pants, and Octavia Spencer, in a vibrant red gown with a magenta cape, who walked onto the purple and white stage to loud applause. Octavia Spencer, holding an envelope, spoke about original and adapted screenplays being "cut from the same cloth," while Melissa McCarthy listened. McCarthy, gesturing with red-painted nails, stated that each art form maintains its individual characteristics, comparing it to themselves, and humorously added, "I don't see us as the like repant pelvic thrust types." Spencer playfully retorted, "Well, speak for yourself," leading to a humorous debate over the pronunciation of "Chippendale" versus "Chip-in-Dale." McCarthy then suggested, "Why don't we just read what the brilliant writers wrote for us?" Spencer exclaimed, "Oh! Look! There's a script on the thing," as McCarthy added, "And it has my name up there. Look at it." Spencer observed, "Oh, it's in yellow," as they continued their lighthearted presentation.

event_blocks.summary: On the Oscars stage, a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, with long dark hair and red-painted nails, announced the nominees for Best Original Screenplay, emphasizing that a writer must "capture the audience's hearts, but simultaneously entertain and surprise." The nominees presented included 'ANATOMY OF A FALL' by Justine Triet and Arthur Harari, featuring a clip of Sandra intensely stating, "You are not a victim. Not at all. Your generosity conceals something dirtier and meaner"; 'THE HOLDOVERS' by David Hemingson, with Kountze saying, "I can't fail this," and Paul responding, "Oh, don't sell yourself short, Mr. Kountze. I truly believe that you can"; 'MAESTRO' by Bradley Cooper & Josh Singer, showing LB emotionally remarking, "Too much, isn't it? Too much. I love too much, what can I say, but I'm reigning it in"; and 'MAY DECEMBER' by Samy Burch and Alex Mechanik, with a clip of Grace stating, "Insecure people are very dangerous, aren't they? I'm secure." The segment also highlighted 'PAST LIVES' by Celine Song, featuring Hae Sung saying, "You disappeared from my life, and I just found you again." Ultimately, the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay was awarded to 'Anatomy of a Fall,' with Justine Triet and Arthur Harari accepting the award. Triet, visibly emotional, humorously noted the award would help her through her 'midlife crisis' and contrasted the current glamour with being 'stuck in a house with two kids' during lockdown, concluding there's 'no line... between work and diapers.' Harari acknowledged producers and humorously described their creative process as 'mixed doubles ping-pong played by neurotic French nerds,' eliciting laughter from the audience, including Jodie Foster, who had presented the award.

event_blocks.summary: Cord Jefferson, holding an Oscar statuette and a card, stood at the Oscars podium, delivering an emotional acceptance speech advocating for smaller films over "$200 million movies" and encouraging the industry to "make 20 $10 million movies" or "50 $4 million movies," emphasizing the joy of filmmaking and the potential of undiscovered talent like the "next Martin Scorsese, Greta (both Gretas), and Christopher Nolan." He expressed gratitude to those who trusted him, a "40-year-old black guy who'd never directed anything before," highlighting the life-changing impact of the opportunity. The stage, illuminated with warm lighting and draped in purple fabric, provided a grand backdrop as the diverse Oscars audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, and the Woman in Sparkling Silver Dress, applauded enthusiastically. The Woman in Red Gown with Magenta Cape was seen embracing Cord Jefferson as he approached the stage and later stood beside him, smiling and clapping. As Cord Jefferson concluded his passionate speech, raising the Oscar and card for emphasis amidst sustained applause, triumphant music swelled. The segment abruptly cut to a woman in a black-and-white checkered jacket speaking into a microphone against a vibrant pink background, a stark contrast to the preceding formal ceremony. Earlier, Dwayne Johnson and Bad Bunny presented the International Feature Film award. Dwayne Johnson, in a silver suit, stated, "Cinema is a universal language that speaks to the common threads that bind us together." Bad Bunny, in a black double-breasted suit, added in Spanish, "El arte del cine es un lenguaje universal que habla a los hilos que nos unen juntos," before translating and humorously remarking, "You see, he is not such a bad bunny. Happy birthday, brother. Gracias, thank you." He then introduced the nominees: IO CAPITANO from Italy, PERFECT DAYS from Japan, SOCIETY OF THE SNOW from Spain, and THE BOY AND THE HERON from Japan, with clips of each film shown under dramatic lighting, showcasing their diverse themes.

event_blocks.summary: The Oscars stage, illuminated by warm, dramatic lighting and a backdrop featuring radiating golden rods and the words 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE', hosted the announcement for the International Feature Film award. Initially, a montage showcased clips from nominees 'The Teachers’ Lounge' (Germany), 'Io Capitano' (Italy), and 'The Zone of Interest' (United Kingdom). Presenters, including The Rock in a silver suit and a man in a black double-breasted suit with glasses, stood on the elevated circular stage. The man announced the nominees before declaring, "And the Oscar goes to... The Zone of Interest!" The audience erupted in cheers as a woman in a black pinstriped sequined suit, visibly emotional, rose and walked down the aisle after hugging a man. The presenter noted that 'The Zone of Interest' was the ninth non-English language film nominated for both International Feature Film and Best Picture in the same year. The woman then appeared on stage, receiving the Oscar from The Rock and the presenter. The winning presenter, speaking with a British accent and wearing glasses, thanked the Academy, partners like A24 and Film4, the Polish Film Institute, and the Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum, as well as his producers, actors, and collaborators. He emphasized the film's purpose to "reflect and confront us in the present," showing "where dehumanization leads at its worst." He continued, "Right now we stand here as men who refute their Jewishness and the Holocaust being hijacked by an occupation which has led to conflict for so many innocent people," a statement met with sustained applause. He further added, "Whether the victims of October the 7th in Israel, or the ongoing attack on Gaza, all are victims of this dehumanization," before posing the question, "How do we resist?" which also garnered strong applause.

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Black Tuxedo, Robert Downey Jr., initially watches expectantly from the audience before his expression shifts to surprise as Ke Huy Quan, the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, presents the 'ACTOR IN A SUPPORTING ROLE' envelope on the grand Oscars Stage, which features a large golden Oscar statue and illuminated blue panels with 'SUPPORTING ACTOR' displayed. Ke Huy Quan praises the nominee's extraordinary and transformative performance in American Fiction, stating, "The magic you bring on screen, it's outmatched only by the magic of who you are as a person," causing Robert Downey Jr.'s eyes to widen further. The audience erupts in applause and cheers, with the Woman in White Dress beside Robert Downey Jr. clapping enthusiastically, and Robert Downey Jr. stands, looking stunned. Ke Huy Quan then jokes on stage about actors who don't drop character until the DVD commentary, specifically naming Robert Downey Jr. and referring to him as "Susan's husband," prompting laughter and applause from the celebratory and electric audience. Later, the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses is shown on a split-screen, gesturing animatedly and praising an actor's versatility, noting their ability to portray 'Iron Man' and 'every man,' learn to play the violin left-handed for Chaplin, and now steal scenes in Oppenheimer, earning a third Oscar nomination for their portrayal of Lewis Strauss, which elicits laughter and applause. The Oscars Stage is wide-shot, illuminated with blue lighting, featuring the golden Oscar statue and 'SUPPORTING ACTOR' above. The Oscars Host, in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, stands on stage with a blue, textured backdrop, clapping and introducing Robert De Niro's performance in Killers of the Flower Moon as a different type of evil, a businessman consumed, which is met with applause and cheers. The Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses and the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress are seen clapping and smiling in the audience, appearing engaged and attentive throughout the host's continued introductions.

event_blocks.summary: The Oscars ceremony featured a split-screen presentation with Robert De Niro, in a black tuxedo and glasses, smiling and nodding in the audience, while Tim Robbins, also in a black tuxedo, animatedly gestured on stage, declaring someone was "consumed by greed and his lust for power" before praising De Niro's "Oscar-worthy performance" and calling him an "acting legend," prompting audience laughter and applause. The scene shifted to a wider shot of the stage with three men in tuxedos clapping before focusing on the gray-haired Oscars Host, who remarked on "certain male behavior" resembling an "inferiority complex of a plastic doll" but then questioned why Ryan Gosling's Ken is admired, suggesting his portrayal allows for connection. Later, a split-screen showed Ryan Gosling, in a black suit with a silver necklace, thoughtfully touching his mouth, while the Oscars Host praised a film as "funny, intelligent, hugely entertaining, and ultimately poignant and deserving of empathy," leading to audience applause. The camera then focused on Gosling clapping warmly next to a Woman in Black-and-Gold Sequined Dress. The Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, wearing a black tuxedo, white shirt, tinted glasses, and a red boutonniere, enthusiastically announced "Mark Ruffalo," commending his "over three decades" of work and "versatility," particularly his "unmatched appetite for challenges" in "Poor Things," which "rightfully earned" him the nomination, drawing loud applause and cheers. The audience, including a Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere, a Woman in Black-and-White, and another Man in Black Tuxedo, reacted positively. A final split-screen showed the Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere and the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, both engaged, with the latter pointing and speaking energetically amidst continued audience applause and cheers, maintaining a formal and celebratory atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere and Sterling K. Brown applaud as the Man in Dark Suit with Glasses, presenting the Supporting Actor award, praises nominee Mark Ruffalo as a wonderful human being. The Man in Dark Suit with Glasses then announces Robert Downey Jr. as the winner, prompting cheers and applause from the Oscars Audience. Robert Downey Jr., visibly surprised and covering his mouth, rushes down the aisle through the audience to the Oscars Stage, where he is handed the Oscar by the announcer, who notes it's his first win and third nomination. On stage, with a digital nameplate reading 'ROBERT DOWNEY JR. ACTOR IN A SUPPORTING ROLE OPPENHEIMER', Robert Downey Jr. begins his acceptance speech, thanking the Academy and humorously noting the presenter only flubbed one line, eliciting laughter. He then expresses gratitude to his wife, Susan Downey, stating she saved him, and acknowledges "Chris," "Emma," and the cast and crew of "Oppenheimer," including Emily, Killian, Matt Damon, and Emily Blunt, for making him a better man, before thanking his publicist and agent.

event_blocks.summary: The Oscars ceremony commenced with a high-energy performance of "Just Ken!" by Ryan Gosling, clad in a sparkling pink sequined suit, accompanied by male dancers in black suits and cowboy hats on a circular stage featuring a prominent 'Ken' logo backdrop and dynamic on-screen prompts. The performance concluded with animated pink mannequin heads swirling on the stage floor, followed by enthusiastic applause from the audience, including Martin Scorsese. A host in a beige patterned tuxedo then humorously presented a pink sequined jacket, jokingly initiating a bidding war before introducing Ariana Grande and Cynthia Erivo to present the Oscar for Original Score and Song. Grande, in a voluminous pink strapless gown, and Da’Vine Joy Randolph, in an emerald green gown, walked onto the stage, flanked by golden arches and a large circular screen displaying film montages. Randolph and Grande delivered poignant remarks about the impact of film music, with Randolph referencing "somewhere over the rainbow" and Grande playfully responding "wickedly subtle." The segment transitioned to a cinematic montage of Original Score nominees, including 'American Fiction,' 'Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny,' 'Killers of the Flower Moon,' 'Oppenheimer,' and 'Poor Things,' each introduced by a female voiceover and accompanied by their respective scores. The Oscar for Original Score was awarded to Ludwig Göransson for 'Oppenheimer.' Göransson, accepting his second Oscar, thanked director Christopher Nolan, his wife Serena Göransson, his children Apollo and Romeo, his music team, and his parents, sharing an anecdote about recording at night and the importance of guitars and drum machines. The ceremony concluded with Göransson on stage, holding his statuette, as the audience applauded and celebratory orchestral music played.

The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, bathed in warm golden lighting, featuring a large circular screen at its center displaying a montage of film scenes. Elegant, curved golden arches flank the stage, while the audience, seated in darkness, focuses their attention forward, their applause and cheers filling the air. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, striking in an emerald green gown with dramatic ruffled shoulders and sparkling earrings, and Ariana Grande, in a voluminous, ruffled pink strapless gown with her blonde hair in an elegant updo, walk onto the stage. They approach the central podium, where Da’Vine Joy Randolph takes the microphone, her clear voice announcing, "We're here to present the Oscar for Original Song and Original Score. A great movie song can set the mood, reveal something about a character, and help us tell a story." Ariana Grande stands smiling beside her, occasionally glancing at her, then adds, "And a great film score can even leave a handprint on our hearts forever. It can ignite wonder and astonishment, make us feel sadness and longing, and can even transport us to new worlds." Da’Vine Joy Randolph continues, "Or somewhere over the rainbow where the dreams that you dare to dream really do come true." Ariana Grande playfully responds, "Oh, how wickedly subtle of you," to which Da’Vine Joy Randolph replies, "I do try." As Da’Vine Joy Randolph speaks, the circular screen behind them transitions to a split-screen montage of scenes from 'American Fiction,' including a woman in a white blouse and large sunglasses, a man in a white shirt walking by the ocean, and a woman in a white dress. The on-screen text 'AMERICAN FICTION' and 'LAURA KARPMAN' appears at the bottom. Da’Vine Joy Randolph then announces, "And now, here are the nominees for Original Score," followed by a voiceover stating, "American Fiction by Laura Karpman," as the visual tribute continues.

A stylized piano-key visual effect introduces 'INDIANA JONES AND THE DIAL OF DESTINY' by John Williams, as a female voice announces the film and composer, showing an older man examining a golden artifact and a vintage car. This transitions to 'KILLERS OF THE FLOWER MOON' by Robbie Robertson, also announced by the female voice, depicting a man in a suit in a 1920s town and a dimly lit saloon. Next, 'OPPENHEIMER' by Ludwig Göransson is presented, with the female voice announcing it, showing a man in a checkered jacket in a car and a serious man in a suit. Finally, 'POOR THINGS' by Jerskin Fendrix is introduced by the female voice, featuring a woman in a yellow dress in an ornate interior and a man holding a glass. The montage concludes with a grand reveal of the 'ORIGINAL SCORE' category on a golden, circular stage. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, in a striking emerald green gown, and Ariana Grande, in a voluminous pink strapless gown, stand at the podium. Da’Vine Joy Randolph announces, "And the Oscar goes to..." before Ariana Grande reveals, "Oppenheimer. Ludwig Göransson." The audience erupts in applause and cheers as Ludwig Göransson, dressed in a black suit, rises from his seat, smiling and clapping. He walks down the aisle through the cheering crowd to the stage, where he is greeted by the presenters and joyfully receives the Oscar statuette. A male announcer's voice highlights, "This is a second Oscar win and third nomination for Ludwig Göransson, who took home his first Oscar in this category for Black Panther," as the audience continues to applaud.

The segment opens to the sound of loud applause and cheering as Ludwig Göransson, dressed in a sleek black tuxedo, walks onto the Oscars stage holding his golden Oscar statuette. He approaches the podium with a joyful, slightly overwhelmed expression, smiling broadly, and exclaims "Wow" and "Um" before beginning his acceptance speech. He thanks Christopher Nolan and Emma Thomas for inviting him on this "incredible world," crediting Nolan for the idea to use a violin in the score, which allowed him to collaborate with his "wonderful wife and acclaimed violinist, Serena Göransson." The audience responds with another round of applause. Göransson continues, sharing a personal anecdote about recording at night and rushing home to put their kids, Apollo and Romeo, down to bed, noting that the result set a "really nice tone for the film." As he speaks, the camera cuts to the audience, showing a man in a black tuxedo with glasses clapping and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress smiling. The camera returns to Göransson, who uses expressive hand gestures, occasionally glancing at the audience. A lower-third graphic identifies him as 'LUDWIG GÖRANSSON, ORIGINAL SCORE, OPPENHEIMER.' He then thanks his music team—Teresa Stanislav, Jake Brown, Chris Fogel, Anthony Parther, and Alyssa Park—for making the music sound great, and emotionally thanks his parents for giving him "guitars and drum machines instead of video games." He concludes with a heartfelt "Thank you!" as the camera pulls back to a wide shot, revealing Da’Vine Joy Randolph in a striking emerald green gown and Ariana Grande in a voluminous, ruffled pink strapless gown standing beside him on stage. The audience is shown clapping and cheering loudly, accompanied by celebratory orchestral music, as Göransson finishes his speech, smiling and nodding appreciatively before stepping back from the podium, the golden stage lighting enhancing the grandeur of the moment.

event_blocks.summary: Billie Eilish, visibly emotional and wearing a black tuxedo with a red pin, accepted an Oscar statuette on a grand stage bathed in warm golden lighting, thanking her best friend Zoe, dance and choir teachers, and concluding with 'Thank you to the Academy. I love you.' Beside her, Finneas O’Connell, in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, held his Oscar, smiling and thanking friends and Joe Marianelli. The Oscars Host, in a beige patterned tuxedo, announced the 'Best Actor' category and introduced the original members of NSYNC to present. The stage, now bathed in purple lighting with 'LEAD ACTOR' illuminated above a golden Oscar statue, featured flowing purple drapes and floral arrangements as the engaged audience watched. The 'LEAD ACTOR' nominees were displayed on five large vertical screens, including humorous depictions of Nicolas Cage, Matthew McConaughey, Brendan Fraser, Paul Giamatti, and Barack Obama. The Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses and the Man in Dark Suit walked onto the stage as the audience applauded. A voice listed nominees: 'Nicolas Cage, Matthew McConaughey, Brendan Fraser, Ben Kingsley, Forest Whitaker,' followed by applause. The screens then showed Paul Giamatti, Harrison Ford, Cord Jefferson, Cillian Murphy, and Samuel L. Jackson. The Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses praised Jeffrey Wright's performance in 'American Fiction,' calling him 'funny and furious and flawed and empathetic,' which elicited cheers. Nicolas Cage spoke animatedly about actors going 'above and beyond,' specifically praising Paul Giamatti's commitment. The Man in Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere introduced Bradley Cooper for 'Maestro,' highlighting his writing, directing, producing, and acting. Adam Sandler, seated next to a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress with sunglasses, smiled and clapped. Matthew McConaughey praised Bradley Cooper's transformative journey into Leonard Bernstein. Ben Kingsley then announced Cillian Murphy as the winner, describing his portrayal in 'Oppenheimer' as 'masterful' with 'layers of humanity' while his character created 'something inhuman,' calling it 'riveting to watch,' leading to loud cheers and applause from the audience.

event_blocks.summary: On the Oscars stage, bathed in golden light with a large Oscar statue and floral arrangements, four men in tuxedos stood as orchestral music played and the audience applauded. Jeffrey Wright, wearing a patterned tuxedo, addressed Colman Domingo, praising his "bold, brilliant" portrayal of Bayard Rustin and acknowledging his "genius as an actor." The camera cut to Colman Domingo in the audience, who adjusted his bow tie with a smile. Following enthusiastic applause, a split-screen showed Wright and Domingo, emphasizing their connection. The 'LEAD ACTOR' sign illuminated above the stage as a presenter in a black tuxedo revealed the nominees: Jeffrey Wright, Paul Giamatti, Bradley Cooper, Cillian Murphy, and Colman Domingo. Cillian Murphy reacted with visible surprise and animation. The presenter announced, "And the Oscar goes to... Cillian Murphy!" for Best Actor in a Leading Role for 'Oppenheimer,' prompting loud applause and cheers. Murphy, in a classic black tuxedo, showed wide-eyed disbelief before rising, embracing Greta Gerwig in a sparkling silver gown, and hugging Robert Downey Jr. as he walked toward the stage. The presenter noted it was Murphy's first Oscar win and nomination. On stage, after receiving the Oscar statuette, Murphy, visibly emotional, began his acceptance speech, thanking "the Academy," "Chris Nolan and Emma Thomas," and describing the journey as "wildest" and "exhilarating." He expressed gratitude to the Oppenheimer cast and crew, acknowledged fellow nominees, and thanked his "incredible team" and family, including his partner Yvonne McGuinness and sons Malachy and Aran, eliciting cheers. Declaring himself a "very proud Irishman," Murphy dedicated his award "to the peacemakers everywhere" as the crowd erupted in cheers and applause, celebratory music swelled, and he raised his arm in triumph.

Four men in tuxedos stand on stage, as upbeat, celebratory orchestral music plays and the audience applauds loudly. The focus shifts to Jeffrey Wright, a bald man with glasses and a beard, wearing a black patterned tuxedo, who speaks expressively with hand gestures, addressing Colman Domingo directly: "Colman. When I first worked with you... a decade or two ago. I saw that you were bold, brilliant, bound for greatness. It's been exciting to watch you light up the screen over the years, most recently with your truly inspired portrayal of civil rights leader Bayard Rustin." The camera cuts to the audience, highlighting Colman Domingo, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and a gold brooch, who is seated and adjusts his bow tie with a subtle smile, while others around him watch attentively. Jeffrey Wright continues, "Colman, your genius as an actor illuminates the genius of an angelic troublemaker who's nearly buried in the history books. Thankfully, Justin is getting his due, as are you. Colman Domingo." A wave of enthusiastic audience applause and cheers erupts, accompanied by swelling orchestral music, as a split-screen then shows Jeffrey Wright on stage and Colman Domingo in the audience, emphasizing their connection. The scene transitions to a wide view of the stage with the illuminated sign 'LEAD ACTOR' above, as the audience is shown in full. The camera then focuses on a presenter in a black tuxedo holding a card, standing beside a graphic displaying the five nominees for 'Actor in a Leading Role': Jeffrey Wright, Paul Giamatti, Bradley Cooper, Cillian Murphy, and Colman Domingo, each shown in circular frames with their names below. The nominees are shown reacting in the audience, with Cillian Murphy, a man in a black tuxedo, visibly surprised and animated, while others smile or nod. The presenter's voice is heard saying, "And the Oscar goes to..." as the formal and celebratory atmosphere, with its elegant stage design, enhances the prestige of the moment.

The segment opens with the Presenter in Black Tuxedo declaring "Cillian Murphy!" as the winner for Best Actor in a Leading Role for 'Oppenheimer', immediately triggering a wave of loud, enthusiastic applause and cheers from the Oscars Audience. Cillian Murphy, dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a white pleated shirt and a gold circular lapel pin, reacts with wide-eyed surprise and an open-mouthed expression of disbelief from his seat. As upbeat, celebratory orchestral music begins to play, he rises and is warmly embraced by Greta Gerwig, who is in a sparkling silver gown, smiling and clapping joyfully. He then moves through the standing, applauding audience, hugging Robert Downey Jr., who beams with pride. The Presenter's voiceover notes, "This is the first Oscar win and nomination for Cillian Murphy," as Murphy continues his walk toward the Oscars Stage. He is greeted by the Presenter in Black Tuxedo, who hands him the Oscar statuette. Murphy then walks onto the circular stage, illuminated with purple drapes and a large golden Oscar statue in the background, with a lower-third graphic identifying him as 'CILLIAN MURPHY, ACTOR IN A LEADING ROLE, OPPENHEIMER'. Approaching the podium, he is visibly emotional, placing a hand on his face, and after a soft "Oh" and a slightly overwhelmed "Um, I'm a little overwhelmed," he begins his acceptance speech, gesturing expressively and thanking "the Academy," "Chris Nolan and Emma Thomas," before stating, "It's been the wildest, most..." The audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses and the Man in White Tuxedo, continues to applaud in the celebratory atmosphere.

The segment opens with Cillian Murphy, impeccably dressed in a black tuxedo with a gold lapel pin, delivering an emotional acceptance speech for Lead Actor at the Oscars podium. He expresses profound gratitude to the Oppenheimer cast and crew for an "exhilarating" and "creatively satisfying journey," and acknowledges his fellow nominees with awe. As Murphy speaks, gesturing expressively, the audience, including a smiling Man in Tuxedo with Glasses and a Woman in Black Ruffled Gown, claps in appreciation. He thanks his "incredible team," specifically Craig Bankey, and lovingly names his family: Brendan Murphy, Mary Murphy, Yvonne McGuinness (his partner in life and art), and his sons Malachy and Aran, prompting cheers and applause from the crowd. Murphy proudly declares, "I'm a very proud Irishman standing here tonight," as the camera cuts to a wide shot revealing the grand stage with the illuminated 'LEAD ACTOR' sign. He is joined by presenters and nominees, such as a Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere and a Woman in Silver Beaded Gown, all applauding. The audience, including a joyful Bald Man in Black Tuxedo and a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, claps enthusiastically. Murphy concludes by dedicating his award to "the peacemakers everywhere," referencing the film's subject, as the crowd erupts in cheers and applause, and celebratory music swells, with Murphy raising his arm in triumph under warm, golden lighting.

event_blocks.summary: As triumphant orchestral music swelled, the Oscars stage, adorned with purple drapes and a large golden Oscar statue, was revealed with the audience standing and applauding. Steven Spielberg, wearing a black tuxedo, walked onto the stage from the audience, holding a beige envelope labeled 'DIRECTING'. He spoke into the microphone, stating, "At their very best, films can do more than entertain us. They can open our hearts to each other. They can show us ourselves, and they can shine a light on where we've been and make us think about where we are going." He continued, "We are so fortunate that this has been a great and eclectic year for movies, and a tribute to all the places these five nominated directors have taken the entire world." After announcing Justine Triet for "Anatomy of a Fall" and Martin Scorsese, a montage of behind-the-scenes footage showed Christopher Nolan on the *Oppenheimer* set, Yorgos Lanthimos directing *Poor Things*, and Jonathan Glazer filming *The Zone of Interest*. The Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses, the presenter, announced the nominees for Best Director, ultimately declaring, "And the Oscar goes to... Christopher Nolan, Oppenheimer!" The Oscars Audience erupted in applause as Christopher Nolan rose from his seat, embracing colleagues like Robert Downey Jr. and Cillian Murphy on his way to the stage. Visibly moved, Nolan accepted the Oscar statuette and approached the podium, beginning his acceptance speech by thanking "so many people who've dragged me up here," specifically mentioning Universal Studios and Donna Langley, Kai Bird and Martin Sherwin, his "incredible cast" including Cillian Murphy, the crew, Chuck Roven, Dan Michael, his brother Jonah, his family, and producer Emma Thomas, while Jodie Foster was visibly emotional.

They can open our hearts to each other. They can show us ourselves, and they can shine a light on where we've been and make us think about where we are going." Nolan walks down the aisle, embracing colleagues like Robert Downey Jr. and Cillian Murphy, and is warmly greeted on stage by the presenter with a hug and a kiss. Christopher Nolan, dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a bow tie, stands at the Oscars podium holding his golden Oscar statuette for Best Director for *Oppenheimer*. The stage, adorned with deep purple drapes and a large golden Oscar silhouette, creates a formal and celebratory atmosphere. Nolan speaks with emotion, occasionally gesturing with his free hand while holding the award securely, appearing reflective and grateful. He begins by thanking "so many people who've dragged me up here," specifically mentioning Universal Studios and Donna Langley for "seeing the potential." He then thanks Kai Bird and Martin Sherwin for their 25 years on the book the film is based on. As he continues, the camera alternates between medium shots and close-ups, capturing his expressions. He also thanks the crew, some of whom were "rewarded tonight," and Chuck Roven for "putting the book in my hands." Nolan then thanks those who "believed in me my whole career," including Dan Michael, his brother Jonah, and his family. As he concludes, mentioning "the incredible Emma Thomas, producer," the audience, including Robert Downey Jr., Cillian Murphy, and Greta Gerwig, applauds warmly, with Jodie Foster visibly emotional and wiping tears from her eyes. The warm lighting emphasizes the grandeur of the event, underscoring the significance of this moment of collective appreciation.

event_blocks.summary: Christopher Nolan, holding his Oscar statuette on the Oscars stage adorned with purple drapes and a golden statue, expressed humility and emotion, stating, "Producer of all our films and all our children. I love you. Uh, and and uh, to the Academy, uh, just to say, uh, movies are just a little bit over 100 years old. I mean, imagine being there 100 years into painting or theater. We don't know where this incredible journey is going from here, but to know that you think that I'm a meaningful part of it means the world to me. Thank you very much." Following his speech and audience applause, Robert Downey Jr., in a black tuxedo with a silver lapel pin, clapped warmly next to Greta Gerwig in a sparkling silver beaded gown, while Martin Scorsese, in a black tuxedo, also clapped serenely as upbeat orchestral music began. The scene shifted to a wide shot of the stage displaying the 'LEAD ACTRESS' category above a golden circular design with drapes and floral arrangements, with five large screens showing nominees: one in a black floral dress, another in a teal gown, a third in a sparkling silver dress, a fourth in a black dress with a metallic skirt, and the fifth in a white gown with a high slit, as an announcer declared, "And the Oscar goes to... Sally Field, Jennifer Lawrence, Michelle Yeoh, Charlize Theron, Jessica Lange," prompting the audience to stand and applaud. Later, a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer stood at the podium holding the 'ACTRESS IN A LEADING ROLE' envelope, introducing the nominees displayed on large vertical screens: a Woman in Yellow Dress, a Woman in Plaid Shawl, a Woman in Gray Suit, a Woman in Blue-and-Black Top, and a Woman in Black Dress, specifically highlighting "The amazing Sandra Hüller" (Woman in Yellow Dress) for "Anatomy of a Fall," praising her ambiguous portrayal of a woman accused of killing her husband. The audience, including a Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, reacted with smiles and applause. A woman with gray hair and glasses, wearing a flowing white gown with sheer sleeves, spoke warmly about "Emma Stone's brilliant Bella" in "Poor Things," describing the character as "curious as a puppy and hungry as a bear," "unhinged, uninhibited and completely original," and "unforgettable and endearing," praising Stone as "always surprising" and someone who "refuses to be categorized," concluding with "Bravo, Emma!" The audience, including a woman in a strapless white gown with a diamond necklace, laughed and applauded. Another woman in a black-and-white polka dot dress with a diamond necklace joined the speaker on stage, addressing "Lily" and stating, "you are the soul of Killers of the Flower Moon," met with applause, all within a warm, golden, celebratory atmosphere of anticipation.

event_blocks.summary: The video segment opens with audience close-ups, featuring a woman in a navy blue strapless dress with a blue gemstone necklace and a woman in a black-and-white polka dot off-the-shoulder dress, who are then shown side-by-side. A wide shot reveals four women on the Oscars Stage, including the Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress, in front of a golden backdrop and Oscar statue. The Woman in Silver Satin Gown speaks, praising a performance as "Molly Burkhart," imbued with the "plight, resilience, and spirit of the Osage people," and commending "advocacy offset as a powerful display of dedication and leadership." She then shares a personal anecdote about watching "Annette" portray "Diane Nyad" as an "intense sensory experience." The audience reacts, with a split-screen showing the Woman in Blue Sequined Dress with Orange Glasses and the Woman in Silver Satin Gown smiling. The Woman in Silver Satin Gown continues, stating, "Your work is a beautiful illustration of how to navigate the waves and the ocean and in life." The scene shifts to a wide shot of the Oscars Stage with the 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign, where four nominees, including the Woman in White Gown, the Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress, the Woman in Silver Satin Gown, and the Woman in Black Dress, stand. The Woman in Black Dress steps forward to announce the winner, praising Carey Mulligan's performance in "Maestro" as "radiant" and infusing the role with "unique humanity," declaring Mulligan "so deserving of this honor." Carey Mulligan, seated in the audience, reacts with a subtle smile and gentle claps. The video returns to a split-screen of Carey Mulligan and the Woman in Black Dress. The 'LEAD ACTRESS' category is announced, with Carey Mulligan visibly emotional. The presenter, a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, announces Emma Stone as the winner. Emma Stone erupts in joy, hugging a Man in Black Tuxedo with a Red Boutonniere and another Man in Black Tuxedo before rushing down the aisle to the stage. She is handed the Oscar, with an announcer noting it's her second win and fifth nomination, having previously won for "La La Land." Emma embraces a Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress and other nominees on stage. Emma Stone, on stage, quips about her dress breaking during "I'm Just Ken," eliciting laughter. Her voice trembling, she expresses being overwhelmed and thanks "the women on the stage" and "the women in this category," naming Sandra Hüller, Annette Bening, Carey Mulligan, and Lily Gladstone, and sharing her awe of them. Jodie Foster and Carey Mulligan are shown in the audience, visibly moved. Emma continues her speech, mentioning Yorgos and her family, including her daughter. Later, Emma Stone, holding her Oscar, passionately declares that the win is about a "team that came together to make something greater than the sum of its parts," thanking every cast and crew member and specifically Yorgos for "the gift of a lifetime in Bella Baxter." The camera alternates between Emma and the engaged audience, including a bald man in a tuxedo with glasses and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress, both clapping. A large screen shows Da’Vine Joy Randolph smiling and clapping.

raw_segments: The video segment opens with close-ups of audience members at a formal awards ceremony, focusing first on a woman with long dark hair, wearing a navy blue strapless dress with a white cross-stitch pattern and a prominent blue gemstone necklace, who smiles warmly. The camera then cuts to a woman with blonde hair, wearing a black-and-white polka dot off-the-shoulder dress with a diamond necklace, who looks on with a composed expression. These two are briefly shown side-by-side in a split-screen format. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the stage, where four women stand in front of a large golden Oscar statue and a radiant golden backdrop, with the Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress among them. A woman with blonde hair pulled back, wearing a silver satin one-shoulder gown with a diamond choker and matching earrings, identified as the Woman in Silver Satin Gown, speaks clearly, praising a performance as "Molly Burkhart," noting it is "imbued with the plight, resilience, and spirit of the Osage people." She commends the "advocacy offset as a powerful display of dedication and leadership," before offering "Congratulations." This is met with enthusiastic applause and cheers from the audience. The camera then focuses on the Woman in Silver Satin Gown again as she calls out "Annette," prompting another wave of applause and cheers. She continues, sharing a personal anecdote: "I've never been stung by a jellyfish. But seeing you endure it multiple times made me feel like I had." She describes watching "Annette" portray "Diane Nyad" as an "intense sensory experience," where one can "taste the salt water, feel the sunburn, and relate to the relentless determination." During this, the audience is shown again, highlighting a woman with short blonde hair, wearing orange-tinted glasses, a dark blue sequined dress, and a sheer embroidered shawl, seated next to a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, both smiling and reacting. A split-screen returns, showing the Woman in Blue Sequined Dress with Orange Glasses on the left and the Woman in Silver Satin Gown on the right, both smiling and reacting to the stage. The warm, golden lighting emphasizes the glamour of the event, and the atmosphere is one of anticipation and celebration, punctuated by continuous applause.

The segment opens with a split-screen, showing the Woman in Blue Sequined Dress with Orange Glasses smiling and clapping on the left, while on the right, the Woman in Silver Satin Gown stands on stage, speaking with a composed expression. Her voice is heard delivering a heartfelt message: "Your work is a beautiful illustration of how to navigate the waves and the ocean and in life." The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the grand Oscars Stage, illuminated by the 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign above a golden backdrop featuring a massive Oscar statue. Four nominees, including the Woman in White Gown, the Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress, the Woman in Silver Satin Gown, and the Woman in Black Dress, stand poised on the circular stage amidst a swell of applause and uplifting orchestral music. The camera focuses on the Woman in Black Dress, who steps forward to announce the winner, her expression serious and professional. Her voice then begins to praise the nominee: "In Maestro, Carey Mulligan radiates as Leonard Bernstein's wife Felicia." The scene cuts to the audience, where the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, identified as Carey Mulligan, seated next to the Man in White Tuxedo, reacts with a subtle smile and gentle claps, her expression shifting from anticipation to quiet satisfaction as the speaker continues: "Shining a piercing light on their complicated yet enduring love. But this level of excellence is the norm for Carey. For in every role she takes on, she infuses it with a unique humanity. Without this spectacular performance, Maestro would not have been the movie it became. Carey, you are so deserving of this honor." The video returns to a split-screen, showing Carey Mulligan looking thoughtful on the left, while the Woman in Black Dress on the right speaks with a warm, animated expression, gesturing slightly as she delivers the announcement. The warm, golden lighting throughout emphasizes the grandeur, and the audience's reactions convey a mix of excitement and emotional investment.

The video opens with a close-up of Carey Mulligan, seated in the audience, visibly emotional and wiping her eyes as the 'LEAD ACTRESS' category is announced, accompanied by sustained, building applause. The camera then pulls back to reveal the grand Oscars stage, illuminated with golden drapes and a large 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign above. The presenter, a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, stands at the podium holding the envelope, and the nominees are displayed on screen: Sandra Hüller, Emma Stone, Lily Gladstone, Annette Bening, and Carey Mulligan. The Woman in Black Sequined Blazer clearly announces, "And the Oscar goes to... Emma Stone." As Emma Stone is announced, seated in the audience, she erupts in joy, covering her mouth in disbelief, as loud cheers and triumphant orchestral music immediately erupt. She leaps up, hugging a Man in Black Tuxedo with a Red Boutonniere and then another Man in Black Tuxedo. She runs down the aisle, her strapless mint-green gown flaring as she moves, with the audience cheering and clapping, while the celebratory music continues. She reaches the stage, where she is handed the Oscar by the presenter. An announcer's voice states, "This is a second Oscar win and fifth nomination for Emma Stone. She took home her first Oscar for her leading performance in La La Land. She's the second woman to be nominated for acting and Best Picture for the same film." Emma then embraces a Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress, who is also on stage, before being joined by other nominees, including a Woman in Silver Satin Gown and a Woman in White Gown, as she holds the statuette and smiles triumphantly, all set against the backdrop of continuous applause and the grand orchestral music.

The segment opens with Emma Stone, radiant in a strapless mint-green gown with a flared skirt and a diamond necklace featuring a yellow gem, rushing to the grand Oscars stage adorned with golden drapes and a large golden Oscar statue. Visibly emotional, she clutches her Oscar statuette and immediately quips, "Oh boy, my dress is broken. I think it happened during I'm just Ken," eliciting laughter and applause from the audience. Her voice, trembling with emotion, she admits, "Oh boy, this is really... this is really overwhelming. Sorry, I... okay, my voice is also a little gone. Whatever." As she speaks passionately into the microphone, gesturing expressively, the 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign illuminates above. She praises "the women on the stage" and "the women in this category," then directly addresses the nominees displayed on large vertical screens: "Sandra, Annette, Carey, Lily, I share this with you. I'm in awe of you, and it has been such an honor to do all of this together. I hope we get to keep doing more together." During this heartfelt speech, the camera cuts to the audience, showing emotional reactions, with Jodie Foster in a navy blue satin dress wiping tears and Carey Mulligan, seated in a black off-the-shoulder dress, also visibly moved. Emma continues, "Um, I don't know what I'm saying. Oh my god, I'm totally... okay. The other night, I was panicking, as you can kind of see. Happens a lot. Um, that maybe something like this could happen and and Yorgos said to me, 'Please...'" The celebratory and heartfelt atmosphere is palpable, emphasized by the warm, golden stage lighting.

The minute opens with a close-up of a bearded man in a black tuxedo, seated in the audience, looking intently toward the stage. The camera then cuts to Emma Stone, radiant in a mint-green strapless gown with a flared skirt and a diamond necklace featuring a yellow gem, standing at the Oscars Stage podium, holding her Oscar statuette. With an emotional and animated delivery, her voice slightly cracking, she passionately declares that "it's not about me," but "about a team that came together to make something greater than the sum of its parts," emphasizing that this collaborative spirit is "the best part about making movies." She expresses being "deeply honored to share this with every cast member, with every crew member, with every single person who poured their love and their care and their brilliance into the making of this film," specifically thanking Yorgos for "the gift of a lifetime in Bella Baxter." As she speaks, gesturing with her free hand, the background features golden drapes and a large circular stage design. The camera alternates between close-ups of Emma Stone and wide shots showing the engaged audience, including a bald man in a tuxedo with glasses and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress, both clapping and smiling, their applause audible in the background. A large screen above the stage displays a close-up of Da’Vine Joy Randolph in her emerald green gown, also smiling and clapping. The atmosphere is celebratory with warm lighting, and Emma, visibly moved, continues her heartfelt speech, acknowledging her family: "my mom, my brother Spencer, my dad, my husband Dave," and "most importantly, my daughter," as the audience remains engaged and appreciative, their applause swelling.

event_blocks.summary: Emma Stone, holding her Oscar statuette at the Oscars podium in a strapless mint-green gown, delivered an emotional acceptance speech, expressing her love for her daughter and humorously advising, "Don't look at the back of my dress." As orchestral music swelled and applause filled the air, she embraced fellow Lead Actress nominees Sandra Hüller, Carey Mulligan, Lily Gladstone, and Annette Bening on the grand Oscars stage, bathed in warm, golden light with illuminated 'OSCARS' signage and a large golden statue. The camera briefly showed Jodie Foster in the audience, visibly emotional and wiping tears. The nominees then exited the stage together, smiling and hugging. The Oscars Host, in a beige patterned tuxedo, then confidently walked onto the stage, humorously remarking about tearing up the envelope to avoid confusion and engaging the audience with expressive hand movements. He acknowledged the focus should be on the actors and filmmakers, mentioning having "an extra minute" before checking his phone. Later, the host, intently checking his smartphone on the warmly lit golden stage, began reading a "review" of himself, humorously asking, "Has there ever been a worse host than Jimmy Kimmel at the Oscars?" eliciting laughter. He continued reading the scathing review, which described his opening as "that of a less than average person trying too hard to be something which he is not and never can be," further amusing the crowd. The camera pulled back to reveal the grand Oscars Stage with a massive golden Oscar statue flanked by pink blossoms. The host suggested replacing Kimmel with "another washed up but cheap ABC talent, George Stephanopoulos," drawing more laughter. He concluded the "review" with "He would make everybody on stage look bigger, stronger, and more glamorous. Blah blah blah, Make America Great Again," prompting a burst of laughter and applause from the audience, including visible reactions from Robert Downey Jr., Cillian Murphy, and Greta Gerwig. The host then challenged the audience to guess who posted the review on Truth Social, revealing, "Well, thank you, President Trump," and quipping, "Thank you for watching. I'm surprised you're still... Isn't it past your jail time?", which caused the audience to erupt in loud, sustained laughter and enthusiastic applause, creating a lively and celebratory atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: Amidst loud applause, cheering, and fanfare-like orchestral music, the Oscars Host, in a beige patterned tuxedo, confidently took the stage, adjusting his bow tie and gesturing expressively to begin his monologue, humorously offering time for anyone who hadn't given a speech, which elicited audience laughter. The grand Oscars stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue flanked by pink floral arrangements and warm golden lighting, was revealed as the Host announced Al Pacino to present the final award, commemorating The Godfather Part 2's 50th anniversary. The Oscars Audience, including Robert Downey Jr. and Cillian Murphy, stood and applauded enthusiastically with continued cheering and dramatic orchestral music as Al Pacino walked across the stage toward the podium. Tim Robbins, in a dark suit and glasses, then walked to the podium, holding a card and microphone, greeting the audience with an emotional "Oh, my friends, thank you, thank you," and playfully suggesting doing Shakespeare before announcing the final award for Best Picture. After building suspense, Robbins revealed the golden envelope and declared, "And the Oscar is... Oppenheimer!" as enthusiastic applause erupted, followed by a joyful "Yes! Yes!" and mentions of Emma Thomas and Charles Roven. Triumphant orchestral music swelled as Christopher Nolan, in a black tuxedo, rose from his seat with his Oscar statuette, followed by Cillian Murphy, Robert Downey Jr., and Emma Stone, all clapping and celebrating as they walked down the aisle, receiving enthusiastic applause and hugs from attendees like Martin Scorsese. Tim Robbins continued to list Oppenheimer's wins, including Actor in a Supporting Role, Film Editing, Cinematography, Original Score, Actor in a Leading Role, Directing, and Best Picture, as the group ascended the grand, golden-lit stage. Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown, stepped forward to the podium, exclaiming "Wow! Oh my goodness! Wow!" and thanking Chris Nolan as "singular" and "brilliant," emphasizing collaboration and teamwork. She also thanked the crew, Universal, IMAX, and all the theaters, her children, her mother-in-law Christina, and her dad, prompting sustained applause. Christopher Nolan then stepped up to the microphone to begin his own thanks to the Academy.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a smiling blonde woman in a black sequined strapless dress, laughing joyfully amidst the sound of loud applause and cheering, accompanied by fanfare-like orchestral music. The scene quickly transitions to the Oscars stage, where the Oscars Host, dressed in a beige patterned tuxedo with a black bow tie, walks confidently onto the stage, adjusting his bow tie and gesturing expressively as he begins his monologue, stating, "It's focused on the important stuff for sure." He then humorously asks, "Before we give out the final award, did anyone uh not get a chance to give a speech? We do have some time if you have anything you want to say. Just line up right here, we'll make sure everybody gets a chance to talk," eliciting audience laughter. The camera pulls back to reveal the grand Oscars Stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue flanked by pink floral arrangements and warm golden lighting, as the Host continues speaking with animated hand gestures, clapping and engaging the audience. He announces, "To present our final award of the evening, and to commemorate the 50th anniversary of The Godfather Part 2... a man who created some of the most iconic characters in movie history. He's an Oscar winner and a nine-time nominee, and we are so glad he was able to find a sitter so he could be here tonight. With the award for Best Picture, please welcome Al Pacino!" The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the auditorium, showing the Oscars Audience standing and applauding enthusiastically with continued cheering and the onset of intense, dramatic orchestral music, as Al Pacino, a man in a dark suit, walks across the stage toward the podium. The stage is illuminated with golden lights, and the audience is visibly enthusiastic, clapping and cheering as Pacino approaches the center of the stage, poised to deliver his acceptance speech. The atmosphere is celebratory and electric, with the golden hues of the stage and the audience’s energy creating a sense of grandeur and excitement, underscored by the powerful orchestral score.

The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, bathed in warm golden light, featuring a large golden Oscar statue flanked by elegant pink floral arrangements. Tim Robbins, a man in a dark suit and glasses, walks confidently to the podium, holding a card and microphone. He begins his speech, greeting the audience with an emotional "Oh, my friends, thank you, thank you," as the Oscars Audience, including Robert Downey Jr., Cillian Murphy, and Greta Gerwig, claps, smiles, and laughs at his playful suggestion of doing Shakespeare, saying "to be... No, I'm not gonna do it." Robbins continues with animated gestures, announcing, "Well, this is the time, uh, for the last award of the evening," and expressing his honor to present it. He speaks with emotion, placing his hands on his chest, as he reveals that "ten wonderful films, uh, were nominated, but only one will take the award for Best Picture," holding up a card labeled 'BEST PICTURE'. The audience remains attentive as he builds suspense, stating, "And, uh, I have to go to the envelope for that. And I will," before revealing a golden envelope and declaring, "Here it comes," to a ripple of anticipation.

The video opens with Tim Robbins, dressed in a dark suit, standing at a podium on a golden-lit stage, holding a golden envelope and reading from a card, his clear voice announcing "And the Oscar is... Oppenheimer!" as enthusiastic applause immediately erupts. He affirms the win with a joyful "Yes! Yes!" and mentions "Emma Thomas, Charles Roven," followed by a lighthearted "What happened?" which elicits laughter from the audience, and a final "Wow." Triumphant orchestral music begins to swell as Christopher Nolan, in a black tuxedo, rises from his seat, holding his Oscar statuette, and begins walking down the aisle. He is followed by a group of cast and crew members, including Cillian Murphy, Robert Downey Jr., and Emma Stone, all in formal attire, clapping and celebrating. As they make their way through the audience, they are greeted with enthusiastic applause and hugs from fellow attendees, including Martin Scorsese, who is seen smiling and clapping. Amidst the continuous applause and swelling music, Tim Robbins continues to list Oppenheimer's impressive wins: "Oppenheimer has 13 nominations and has won seven Oscars tonight for Actor in a Supporting Role, Film Editing, Cinematography, Original Score, Actor in a Leading Role, Directing, and Best Picture." The group ascends the grand, golden-lit stage, which features a large golden Oscar statue backdrop and the words 'BEST PICTURE' illuminated above, gathering around the podium, embracing each other and sharing joyful moments. The audience continues to cheer with sustained, roaring applause as the winners celebrate their victory, with the camera capturing wide shots of the entire auditorium filled with clapping guests, all enveloped in an electric atmosphere of excitement and camaraderie.

The triumphant cast and crew of *Oppenheimer*, all in formal black-tie attire, gather on a grand circular stage adorned with golden drapes and pink floral arrangements, set against a massive golden Oscar statue. Emma Thomas, radiant in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, steps forward to the podium, clutching her Oscar statuette. With an emotional and joyful voice, she exclaims "Wow! Oh my goodness! Wow!" as the audience cheers loudly. She shares her long-held dream of this moment, admitting it seemed unlikely to ever happen, and now standing there, her mind has gone blank. Flanked by Charles Roven and Christopher Nolan, both in classic black tuxedos and holding their own Oscars, Emma declares that the reason the movie was what it was, was "Chris Nolan." The crowd erupts in cheers as she praises him as "singular" and "brilliant," expressing her deep gratitude to him. The on-screen graphic identifies the winners as 'EMMA THOMAS, CHARLES ROVEN, CHRISTOPHER NOLAN' for 'BEST PICTURE OPPENHEIMER'. Emma continues, emphasizing that the fantastic thing about their jobs is the collaboration and teamwork, thanking their "incredible" team, both cast and crew. The atmosphere is celebratory, with the audience's continuous clapping and cheering, and the stage bathed in warm golden light, highlighting the beaming faces of the group as the camera pans across them, capturing their shared elation and pride.

The video captures a triumphant moment on the grand Oscars stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue backdrop flanked by pink floral arrangements and a circular illuminated platform, all bathed in warm golden lighting. Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, delivers an emotional acceptance speech, her voice filled with gratitude and a touch of humor. Holding an Oscar statuette at the podium, she thanks the crew, Universal, IMAX (specifically Rich Gelfond and everyone else at IMAX) for believing in the movie, and all the theaters, stating that as a moviegoer and filmmaker, 'that's why we do what we do.' She then emotionally thanks her children, Flora, Rory, Oliver, and Magnus (who is not present), and acknowledges UK Mother's Day by remembering her late mother and thanking her mother-in-law Christina, who is present with her dad. Finally, she exclaims her honor and thanks the Academy, prompting a wave of loud, sustained applause and cheering from the audience. Flanked by Christopher Nolan, who smiles warmly in his black tuxedo and holds his own Oscar, and Charles Roven, also in a tuxedo and holding an Oscar, the group behind them, including a bald man with a gray beard and glasses, all in formal attire and some holding Oscars, celebrates the win. As the audience continues to cheer, Christopher Nolan then steps up to the microphone, beginning his own thanks to the Academy in a calm, clear voice.

event_blocks.summary: On the grand Oscars stage, bathed in golden light with a massive golden Oscar statue backdrop and pink floral arrangements, Martin Scorsese, wearing a black tuxedo and glasses, emotionally held a golden Oscar statuette and thanked Universal Pictures, Donna Langley, and their team, as Robert Downey Jr. and Jodie Foster applauded, with Foster visibly moved. Scorsese expressed gratitude to his family, calling his wife his 'secret weapon,' and thanked James Woods and David Wargo for entrusting him with the book he 'fortunate enough to give to Chris and Emma.' Christopher Nolan, in a black tuxedo, stood beside Scorsese, holding his own Oscar, listening attentively. raw_segments: The minute opens on the grand Oscars stage, adorned with a massive golden Oscar statue backdrop and flanked by cascading pink floral arrangements, bathed in warm golden light. At the center of the circular, illuminated platform, Martin Scorsese, in a black tuxedo and his signature thick-rimmed glasses, holds a golden Oscar statuette and speaks into a microphone with an earnest and emotional expression. He thanks Universal Pictures, Donna Langley, and their team, as the audience, including Robert Downey Jr. smiling warmly and Jodie Foster visibly emotional, claps. Scorsese continues, expressing gratitude to his family and his wife, whom he affectionately calls the 'secret weapon' of his life. He then thanks James Woods and David Wargo for entrusting him with the project's book, which he was 'fortunate enough to give to Chris and Emma.' Christopher Nolan, in a classic black tuxedo, stands beside Scorsese, holding his own Oscar, listening attentively and occasionally glancing at Scorsese with a proud smile. Behind them, Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves, stands with other formally dressed cast and crew members, all holding Oscars and smiling with pride, including a bald man with a gray beard in a tuxedo and a woman in a one-shoulder sequined black dress. Scorsese concludes by reflecting on his 'amazing 20-plus year experience' with 'Chris and Emma,' highlighting their 'five movies over 20 years' and calling them a 'fantastic team,' stating that being part of making movies with them has been 'completely thrilling.' The camera alternates between wide shots of the celebratory group and close-ups of Scorsese, emphasizing the shared triumph and deep respect among these legendary filmmakers and their team.

event_blocks.summary: Amidst an upbeat orchestral score and crowd applause, a male narrator announced Oscar winners. Dave Mullins and Brad Booker accepted the Animated Short Film award for 'War Is Over! Inspired by the Music of John & Yoko.' Nadia Stacey, Mark Coulier, and Josh Weston received the Makeup and Hairstyling award for 'Poor Things.' James Price and Shona Heath accepted the Production Design award for 'Poor Things,' while Holly Waddington accepted the Costume Design award for the same film. Takashi Yamazaki, Kiyoko Shibuya, Masaki Takahashi, and Tatsuji Nojima were honored with the Visual Effects award for 'Godzilla Minus One.' Jennifer Lame accepted the Film Editing award for 'Oppenheimer.' Ben Proudfoot and Kris Bowers received the Documentary Short Film award for 'The Last Repair Shop.' Mstyslav Chernov, Michelle Mizner, and Raney Aronson-Rath accepted the Documentary Feature Film award for '20 Days in Mariupol.' Hoyte van Hoytema accepted the Cinematography award for 'Oppenheimer.' A clip from 'The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar' was shown as the Live Action Short Film award was announced for it. Finally, Tarn Willers and Johnnie Burn accepted the Sound award for 'The Zone of Interest.' The segment concluded with end credits rolling over a gold background, listing producers, director, and writers, accompanied by the continuing celebratory music and fading applause.
